objectives: Prepare and file the first-time BRSR Core report for FY2025-26 in compliance with SEBI's mandatory disclosure requirements for listed Indian entities.
Achieve voluntary alignment with GRI Standards to meet international stakeholder and investor expectations for a steel-sector manufacturer.
Map material BRSR/GRI disclosures to ESRS topical standards to future-proof reporting for European supply-chain due-diligence requirements (CSRD/CSDDD).
Embed TCFD-aligned climate risk and opportunity disclosures (governance, strategy, risk management, metrics & targets) within the annual report narrative.
Establish a repeatable, audit-ready ESG data collection and assurance process across both manufacturing plants and the Mumbai HQ.
Build internal capacity — data owners, controls, and workflows — so that FY2026-27 reporting can be executed with reduced external support.

frameworks_in_scope.rationale: SEBI mandates BRSR filing for the top 1,000 listed entities by market capitalisation (effective FY2022-23) and BRSR Core assurance for the top 150 (phasing to top 1,000 by FY2026-27). As a listed Indian steel manufacturer, Acme Manufacturing Pvt Ltd is in scope. This is the primary regulatory deliverable for the engagement.

GRI Standards are the globally dominant voluntary ESG framework and are widely referenced by institutional investors, ESG rating agencies (MSCI, Sustainalytics), and steel-sector customers. GRI 11 (Mining & Metals) provides sector-specific disclosures directly applicable to steel manufacturing. Voluntary adoption strengthens credibility and satisfies international stakeholder expectations flagged in the client brief.

Although ESRS (EU CSRD) is not directly mandatory for an Indian-incorporated entity, Acme's steel products are likely exported to or used by EU-based customers subject to CSRD/CSDDD value-chain disclosure obligations. Mapping BRSR/GRI outputs to ESRS topical standards (E1 Climate, E2 Pollution, S1 Own Workforce, G1 Governance) now avoids costly re-work when EU customer requests escalate. Scoped as a mapping/gap exercise, not a full ESRS report.

SEBI's BRSR Core incorporates TCFD-aligned climate disclosures, and TCFD is referenced in ESRS E1. Steel is a high-emissions, transition-risk-exposed sector; scenario analysis and physical risk assessment are material. Completing a standalone TCFD disclosure (or TCFD-aligned section in the Annual Report) satisfies investor expectations and provides the analytical foundation for both BRSR and ESRS climate disclosures. Scoped as an integrated workstream, not a separate report.

scope_charter.reporting_boundary: Consolidation approach: Operational Control. Boundary covers all three entities — Pune Plant (site_pune, MH), Chennai Plant (site_chennai, TN), and Mumbai HQ (site_hq, MH) — as 100%-controlled operations of Acme Manufacturing Pvt Ltd. Scope 1 & 2 GHG emissions boundary = operational control. Scope 3 categories to be determined in Phase 3 materiality/requirement planning (at minimum: purchased goods & services [Cat 1], downstream processing [Cat 10], and end-of-life treatment [Cat 12] are expected material for a steel manufacturer). Financial boundary = standalone financials of Acme Manufacturing Pvt Ltd (CIN: U27100MH2008PTC000000). Any subsidiaries, JVs, or associate entities are out of scope unless confirmed otherwise.
out_of_scope: Full ESRS-compliant report (double-materiality assessment to ESRS depth, ESRS XBRL tagging, and statutory assurance under CSRD) — scoped as mapping/gap analysis only.
Consolidated group-level reporting for any subsidiaries or associate companies of Acme Manufacturing Pvt Ltd (to be confirmed via open question).
Scope 3 categories beyond those identified as material in Phase 3 (full 15-category Scope 3 inventory is out of scope for FY2025-26).
Product-level lifecycle assessment (LCA) or Environmental Product Declarations (EPDs).
Social audit / third-party labour-rights audit of the supply chain.
Carbon credit origination, verification, or trading advisory.
Legal or regulatory compliance advice beyond ESG disclosure obligations.

open_questions: BRSR Core assurance tier: Has Acme Manufacturing Pvt Ltd confirmed its market-cap ranking relative to SEBI's phased BRSR Core assurance threshold (top 150 → top 500 → top 1,000)? This determines whether limited assurance is mandatory for FY2025-26 or voluntary.

Group boundary: Does Acme Manufacturing Pvt Ltd have any subsidiaries, joint ventures, or associate companies whose operations should be included within the reporting boundary? The CIN suggests a private limited structure; please confirm whether any group entities exist.

Assurance provider: Has an external limited-assurance provider been appointed or shortlisted? If not, procurement should begin no later than Phase 3 to avoid a bottleneck ahead of the Phase 7 deadline.

ESRS scope depth: The brief requests 'ESRS alignment' — please confirm whether the deliverable is (a) a gap-mapping annex only, or (b) a fuller double-materiality assessment to ESRS depth. The latter would materially increase effort and timeline.

Scope 3 GHG boundary: Which Scope 3 categories does management consider material beyond the three identified (Cat 1 Purchased Goods, Cat 10 Processing of Sold Products, Cat 12 End-of-Life Treatment)? Confirmation is needed before data templates are issued in Phase 3.

GRI Sector Standard applicability: GRI 11 (Mining & Metals) covers iron and steel. Please confirm whether Acme's operations include any mining/extraction activities (e.g., iron ore, coal) that would trigger additional GRI 11 disclosures beyond downstream steel manufacturing.
Reporting language & format: Will the BRSR be filed as a standalone report or embedded in the Annual Report? Is a separate sustainability report (PDF + XBRL) required for BSE/NSE filing?

Data readiness: Has any prior ESG/sustainability data collection been undertaken for FY2024-25 or earlier? Availability of baseline data will significantly affect Phase 4 effort and the Phase 2 materiality baseline.

scored_topics.label: GHG Emissions
scored_topics.rationale: Steel is one of the most carbon-intensive industries globally (~1.8 t CO₂/t steel via BF-BOF route). GHG emissions drive severe environmental impact (climate change, air quality) and face escalating financial exposure via carbon pricing, EU CBAM, transition risk, and investor/lender covenant requirements. BRSR Core mandates Scope 1 & 2 disclosure; TCFD scenario analysis is in scope; ESRS E1 and GRI 305 apply. Sector materiality maps (GRI 11, SASB Iron & Steel) universally flag this as the #1 topic.
likely_disclosures: BRSR Core — Principle 6: Scope 1, 2 & 3 GHG emissions (tCO₂e); emission intensity per tonne of steel
TCFD — Strategy (scenario analysis, transition & physical risk); Metrics & Targets (net-zero pathway, SBTi alignment)
GRI 305-1/2/3 — Direct, energy-indirect, and other indirect GHG emissions
ESRS E1-6 — Gross Scope 1/2/3 GHG emissions and related targets
EU CBAM — Embedded carbon in exported steel products (customer pass-through obligation)

scored_topics.label: Occupational Health & Safety
scored_topics.rationale: Steel manufacturing is among the highest-risk industrial sectors (molten metal, confined spaces, heavy machinery, heat stress). Fatal and serious injury rates are a direct, severe impact on workers and contractors. Financial exposure includes regulatory penalties under Factories Act/OSHA, compensation liabilities, productivity loss, and reputational damage affecting talent attraction and customer/investor relationships. BRSR Core mandates OHS KPIs; GRI 403 and ESRS S1 apply. Impact score is maximum given severity and breadth of workforce affected.
likely_disclosures: BRSR Core — Principle 3: LTIFR, fatalities, near-miss rate, OHS management system coverage (employees & contractors)
GRI 403-2 — Types of injury, injury rate, occupational diseases, fatalities
GRI 403-8 — Workers covered by OHS management system
ESRS S1-14 — Health & safety metrics for own workforce
ESRS S1-15 — Work-life balance and related indicators
Factories Act 1948 / BOCW Act — Statutory accident reporting to Inspector of Factories

scored_topics.label: Water & Effluents
scored_topics.rationale: Integrated steel plants are large freshwater consumers (2–6 m³/t steel) and generate process effluents (phenols, cyanides, heavy metals from coke ovens and pickling). India's water-stressed geography amplifies community and regulatory risk. Financial exposure arises from water tariffs, regulatory shutdowns, and customer/investor ESG screens. BRSR mandates water withdrawal/discharge disclosure; GRI 303/306 and ESRS E3 apply. Slightly below GHG on financial score as carbon pricing is more acute near-term.
likely_disclosures: BRSR Core — Principle 6: Total water withdrawal by source, water intensity per tonne of steel, water recycling rate
GRI 303-3/4/5 — Water withdrawal, discharge, and consumption by source
GRI 306-3/4/5 — Waste generated, diverted, directed to disposal (effluent sludge)
ESRS E3-4 — Water consumption and intensity metrics
ESRS E2-4 — Pollutants discharged to water (heavy metals, COD, phenols)
CPCB/SPCB consent conditions — Effluent discharge limits and zero-liquid-discharge compliance

questionnaire.question: How significant are our Scope 1, 2, and 3 GHG emissions in terms of their contribution to climate change, and how do you rate the urgency of setting science-based reduction targets for our steel operations?
To what extent do you believe carbon pricing mechanisms (e.g., India carbon market, EU CBAM) or transition regulations will materially affect our cost base or market access within the next 3–5 years?
Does our company's GHG emission profile and decarbonisation roadmap influence your investment, lending, or procurement decisions? If yes, what specific metrics or targets do you require?
How important is it that we disclose Scope 3 (value-chain) emissions — including those from iron ore and coal procurement and downstream steel use — in addition to Scope 1 and 2?
How concerned are you about the volume of freshwater our steel plant withdraws from local sources, particularly given water stress in the operating region?
How significant is the risk of effluent discharge (heavy metals, phenols, suspended solids) from our coke ovens, blast furnaces, and pickling lines to local water bodies and communities?
To what degree does our water recycling rate and progress toward zero-liquid-discharge (ZLD) compliance affect your confidence in our regulatory and operational resilience?
What is your assessment of the financial risk to the business from potential regulatory action (CPCB/SPCB) or community opposition related to water use and effluent management?
How do you rate the severity and frequency of occupational health and safety risks — including fatalities, lost-time injuries, and occupational diseases — in our steel manufacturing operations?
To what extent does our OHS performance (LTIFR, fatality rate, near-miss reporting culture) influence your decision to work with, invest in, or supply to our company?
How adequate do you consider our current OHS management systems, training programmes, and contractor safety oversight in preventing serious incidents?
How important is it that we extend the same OHS standards and reporting to contract workers and third-party labour as we do to permanent employees?

requests.label: Diesel consumed — Pune Plant (monthly)
requests.data_definition: Total diesel consumed on-site per month, in litres. INCLUDES diesel used in DG sets and owned/leased on-site mobile equipment. EXCLUDES petrol, fuel in employee personal vehicles, and contractor-owned equipment unless under operational control.
requests.calc_methodology: Diesel litres × density × NCV × CO2/CH4/N2O emission factors → tCO2e (GWP100, gross CV). Aggregated to Scope 1.

requests.label: Diesel consumed — Chennai Plant (monthly)
requests.data_definition: Total diesel consumed on-site per month, in litres. INCLUDES diesel used in DG sets and owned/leased on-site mobile equipment. EXCLUDES petrol, fuel in employee personal vehicles, and contractor-owned equipment unless under operational control.
requests.calc_methodology: Diesel litres × density × NCV × CO2/CH4/N2O emission factors → tCO2e (GWP100, gross CV). Aggregated to Scope 1.

requests.label: Diesel consumed — Mumbai HQ (monthly)
requests.data_definition: Total diesel consumed on-site per month, in litres (primarily backup DG sets at the office). EXCLUDES petrol and fuel in employee personal vehicles. Enter 0 if no diesel consumed.
requests.calc_methodology: Diesel litres × density × NCV × CO2/CH4/N2O emission factors → tCO2e (GWP100, gross CV). Aggregated to Scope 1.

requests.label: Grid electricity — Pune Plant (monthly)
requests.data_definition: Total kWh drawn from the DISCOM per site, per month. EXCLUDES captive/rooftop solar generation and DG-set generation (those are accounted elsewhere). Use billed kWh from the utility bill.
requests.calc_methodology: kWh × location-based regional grid emission factor (CEA) → tCO2e. Aggregated to Scope 2 (location-based).

requests.label: Grid electricity — Chennai Plant (monthly)
requests.data_definition: Total kWh drawn from the DISCOM per site, per month. EXCLUDES captive/rooftop solar generation and DG-set generation. Use billed kWh from the utility bill.
requests.calc_methodology: kWh × location-based regional grid emission factor (CEA) → tCO2e. Aggregated to Scope 2 (location-based).

requests.label: Grid electricity — Mumbai HQ (monthly)
requests.data_definition: Total kWh drawn from the DISCOM for the office per month. EXCLUDES captive/rooftop solar and DG generation. For leased/shared premises, report apportioned kWh per sub-meter or area share. Use billed kWh.
requests.calc_methodology: kWh × location-based regional grid emission factor (CEA) → tCO2e. Aggregated to Scope 2 (location-based).

requests.label: Revenue (audited) — organisation annual
requests.data_definition: Total revenue for FY2025-26 in INR crore, from audited financial statements. Use the same revenue figure disclosed in the annual report (consolidated, unless engagement scope is standalone).
requests.calc_methodology: GHG intensity = total Scope 1 + Scope 2 (location-based) tCO2e ÷ revenue (INR crore).

requests.label: Groundwater withdrawn — Pune Plant (monthly)
requests.data_definition: Volume of groundwater withdrawn via borewells per site, per month, in kL. Measured at borewell flow meters. EXCLUDES municipal/third-party water, tanker water, and rainwater harvesting.
requests.calc_methodology: Summed by source into total water withdrawal (kL).

requests.label: Groundwater withdrawn — Chennai Plant (monthly)
requests.data_definition: Volume of groundwater withdrawn via borewells per site, per month, in kL. Measured at borewell flow meters. EXCLUDES municipal/third-party water, tanker water, and rainwater harvesting.
requests.calc_methodology: Summed by source into total water withdrawal (kL).

requests.label: Municipal water — Pune Plant (monthly)
requests.data_definition: Volume of municipal/third-party (incl. tanker) water received per site, per month, in kL. Per water-utility bills/tanker invoices. EXCLUDES groundwater and rainwater.
requests.calc_methodology: Summed by source into total water withdrawal (kL).

requests.label: Municipal water — Chennai Plant (monthly)
requests.data_definition: Volume of municipal/third-party (incl. tanker) water received per site, per month, in kL. Per water-utility bills/tanker invoices. EXCLUDES groundwater and rainwater.
requests.calc_methodology: Summed by source into total water withdrawal (kL).

requests.label: Municipal water — Mumbai HQ (monthly)
requests.data_definition: Volume of municipal/third-party (incl. tanker) water received at the office per month, in kL. Per water-utility bills. For shared premises, report apportioned volume. EXCLUDES groundwater and rainwater.
requests.calc_methodology: Summed by source into total water withdrawal (kL).

requests.label: Effluent discharged — Pune Plant (monthly)
requests.data_definition: Volume of effluent/wastewater discharged per site, per month, in kL, measured at the ETP outlet meter. INCLUDES discharge to environment/sewer/third party. EXCLUDES water recycled and reused within the plant.
requests.calc_methodology: Summed by destination into total water discharge (kL); consumption = withdrawal − discharge.

requests.label: Effluent discharged — Chennai Plant (monthly)
requests.data_definition: Volume of effluent/wastewater discharged per site, per month, in kL, measured at the ETP outlet meter. INCLUDES discharge to environment/sewer/third party. EXCLUDES water recycled and reused within the plant.
requests.calc_methodology: Summed by destination into total water discharge (kL); consumption = withdrawal − discharge.

requests.label: Lost-time injuries — Pune Plant (quarterly)
requests.data_definition: Count of lost-time injuries (LTIs) recorded per site per quarter (employees + workers under operational control). An LTI is a work-related injury resulting in ≥1 lost day beyond the day of incident. Record fatalities separately in the dedicated field. EXCLUDES first-aid-only cases.
requests.calc_methodology: LTIFR = (LTI count × 1,000,000) ÷ hours worked. Fatalities summed across the period.

requests.label: Lost-time injuries — Chennai Plant (quarterly)
requests.data_definition: Count of lost-time injuries (LTIs) recorded per site per quarter (employees + workers under operational control). An LTI is a work-related injury resulting in ≥1 lost day beyond the day of incident. Record fatalities separately. EXCLUDES first-aid-only cases.
requests.calc_methodology: LTIFR = (LTI count × 1,000,000) ÷ hours worked. Fatalities summed across the period.

requests.label: Lost-time injuries — Mumbai HQ (quarterly)
requests.data_definition: Count of lost-time injuries (LTIs) recorded for the office per quarter (employees + workers under operational control). An LTI is a work-related injury resulting in ≥1 lost day. Record fatalities separately. EXCLUDES first-aid-only cases. Enter 0 where none.
requests.calc_methodology: LTIFR = (LTI count × 1,000,000) ÷ hours worked. Fatalities summed across the period.

requests.label: Total hours worked — Pune Plant (quarterly)
requests.data_definition: Total actual hours worked per site per quarter by employees and workers under operational control, from attendance/payroll records. EXCLUDES paid leave, holidays and unworked overtime. Used as the LTIFR denominator.
requests.calc_methodology: LTIFR = (LTI count × 1,000,000) ÷ total hours worked.

requests.label: Total hours worked — Chennai Plant (quarterly)
requests.data_definition: Total actual hours worked per site per quarter by employees and workers under operational control, from attendance/payroll records. EXCLUDES paid leave, holidays and unworked overtime. Used as the LTIFR denominator.
requests.calc_methodology: LTIFR = (LTI count × 1,000,000) ÷ total hours worked.

requests.label: Total hours worked — Mumbai HQ (quarterly)
requests.data_definition: Total actual hours worked for the office per quarter by employees and workers under operational control, from attendance/payroll records. EXCLUDES paid leave, holidays and unworked overtime. Used as the LTIFR denominator.
requests.calc_methodology: LTIFR = (LTI count × 1,000,000) ÷ total hours worked.

report_sections.title: Company Overview
report_sections.body_markdown:
## Company Overview

**Entity:** Acme Manufacturing Pvt Ltd (CIN: U27100MH2008PTC000000)

**Sector:** Iron & Steel Manufacturing

**Reporting Period:** FY2025-26 (1 April 2025 – 31 March 2026)

**Reporting Boundary:** Operational Control. The boundary covers three 100%-controlled operating entities:

| Site | Location | State |
|---|---|---|
| Pune Plant (site_pune) | Pune | Maharashtra |
| Chennai Plant (site_chennai) | Chennai | Tamil Nadu |
| Mumbai HQ (site_hq) | Mumbai | Maharashtra |

Financial data referenced in this report is drawn from the standalone financials of Acme Manufacturing Pvt Ltd. Revenue for FY2025-26: **₹1,250 crore**. Production volume: **84,000 tonnes** of steel.

> **Note to reviewer:** Chennai Plant (site_chennai) and Mumbai HQ (site_hq) activity data were not included in the FY2025-26 Phase 6 calculation run. All quantitative metrics in this report currently reflect site_pune only. Data collection for the remaining sites must be completed before this report can be filed. This is flagged as a critical open item (see Section 14).

**Frameworks in Scope:**
- **BRSR** (mandatory — SEBI listed-entity requirement)

report_sections.title: ESG Strategy & Governance
report_sections.body_markdown:
## ESG Strategy & Governance

### Strategic Intent

Acme Manufacturing Pvt Ltd is committed to embedding environmental, social, and governance (ESG) considerations into its core business strategy. As an integrated steel manufacturer operating in one of the most carbon- and resource-intensive industrial sectors, the Company recognises that its long-term licence to operate, access to capital, and customer relationships are increasingly contingent on credible, transparent, and improving ESG performance.

This FY2025-26 report marks the Company's **first-time BRSR Core filing** and establishes the baseline data infrastructure, governance accountabilities, and reporting processes that will underpin continuous improvement through FY2026-27 and beyond.

### Governance Structure

> **⚠ Placeholder — Legal/Compliance Review Required:** The following governance narrative must be validated and expanded by management before publication. Board committee names, ESG policy references, and individual role titles should be confirmed against the Company's constitutional documents and board resolutions.

The Board of Directors holds ultimate accountability for ESG strategy and risk oversight. [*Insert: name of Board committee with ESG/sustainability mandate, e.g. Risk & Sustainability Committee*] meets [*insert frequency*] to review material ESG risks and performance against targets. Executive accountability for ESG data quality and disclosure rests with [*insert: CFO / Chief Sustainability Officer / designated BRSR signatory*].

Internal data owners have been designated for each material topic and site, with data flows governed by the ESG data collection protocol established during this engagement.

### Reporting Objectives (FY2025-26)

1. Prepare and file the first-time BRSR Core report in compliance with SEBI's mandatory disclosure requirements.
2. Achieve voluntary alignment with GRI Standards to meet international stakeholder and investor expectations.
3. Map material BRSR/GRI disclosures to ESRS topical standards to future-proof reporting for European supply-chain due-diligence requirements (CSRD/CSDDD).
4. Embed TCFD-aligned climate risk and opportunity disclosures within the Annual Report narrative.
5. Establish a repeatable, audit-ready ESG data collection and assurance process across all three sites.
6. Build internal capacity — data owners, controls, and workflows — so that FY2026-27 reporting can be executed with reduced external support.

report_sections.title: Materiality Assessment
report_sections.body_markdown:
## Materiality Assessment

A structured materiality assessment was conducted to identify and prioritise the ESG topics most significant to Acme Manufacturing Pvt Ltd's business and stakeholders. Topics were scored on two axes — **Impact Score** (severity and breadth of environmental/social impact) and **Financial Score** (magnitude of financial risk or opportunity to the business) — each on a scale of 1–5. Topics scoring above the materiality threshold of **3.5** on either axis are considered material.

### Materiality Matrix Summary

| Rank | Topic | Impact Score | Financial Score | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GHG Emissions | 5 / 5 | 5 / 5 | **Material** |
| 2 | Occupational Health & Safety | 5 / 5 | 4 / 5 | **Material** |
| 3 | Water & Effluents | 4 / 5 | 4 / 5 | **Material** |

*All three topics exceed the materiality threshold of 3.5 on both axes.*

### Topic Rationales

**GHG Emissions (Rank 1 — Impact: 5, Financial: 5)**
Steel manufacturing is among the most carbon-intensive industries globally, with blast furnace–basic oxygen furnace (BF-BOF) routes emitting approximately 1.8 tCO₂ per tonne of steel. GHG emissions drive severe environmental impact through climate change and air quality degradation, and face escalating financial exposure via carbon pricing mechanisms, the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), transition risk, and investor/lender covenant requirements. BRSR Core mandates Scope 1 & 2 disclosure; TCFD scenario analysis, ESRS E1, and GRI 305 are all in scope. Sector materiality maps (GRI 11 Mining & Metals, SASB Iron & Steel) universally identify this as the highest-priority topic.

**Occupational Health & Safety (Rank 2 — Impact: 5, Financial: 4)**
Steel manufacturing is among the highest-risk industrial sectors, with hazards including molten metal handling, confined spaces, heavy machinery, and heat stress. Fatal and serious injury rates represent a direct, severe impact on workers and contractors. Financial exposure includes regulatory penalties under the Factories Act 1948 and OSHA-equivalent statutes, compensation liabilities, productivity loss, and reputational damage affecting talent attraction and customer/investor relationships. BRSR Core mandates OHS KPIs; GRI 403 and ESRS S1 apply.

**Water & Effluents (Rank 3 — Impact: 4, Financial: 4)**
Integrated steel plants are large freshwater consumers (typically 2–6 m³ per tonne of steel) and generate process effluents containing phenols, cyanides, and heavy metals from coke ovens and pickling lines. India's water-stressed geography amplifies community and regulatory risk. Financial exposure arises from water tariffs, potential regulatory shutdowns, and customer/investor ESG screens. BRSR mandates water withdrawal and discharge disclosure; GRI 303/306 and ESRS E3 apply.

### Stakeholder Engagement

The materiality questionnaire covered both internal stakeholders (management, operations, finance) and external stakeholders (investors, customers, lenders, community representatives). Key questions addressed the urgency of science-based GHG targets, carbon pricing exposure, water stress risk, effluent management, and OHS system adequacy. Full questionnaire responses are available in the supporting workpapers.

> **⚠ Placeholder — Management Review Required:** Confirm that the materiality assessment methodology and topic rankings have been reviewed and approved by the Board or designated committee before publication. Document the stakeholder engagement process (number of respondents, engagement method, dates) in the supporting annex.

> **⚠ Placeholder:** BRSR P6 Q3 and GRI 303-4/303-5 also require water discharge volumes (by destination) and net water consumption (withdrawal minus discharge). These figures were not included in the FY2025-26 data run. Provide discharge volumes and treatment method. Assign to: [Data Owner — Environment, site_pune].

### ESRS E3-4 Note

ESRS E3-4 requires disclosure of water consumption (not just withdrawal) and, where applicable, water consumption in areas of high water stress. The 3,250 kL municipal withdrawal figure satisfies the withdrawal component. Net consumption and water-stress area classification (per WRI Aqueduct or equivalent) are required to complete ESRS E3-4.

### Contextual Note — Materiality

As noted in the materiality assessment (Section 3), water and effluents rank third among Acme's material ESG topics, with an impact score of 4/5 and a financial score of 4/5. Integrated steel plants typically consume 2–6 m³ of water per tonne of steel. Acme's FY2025-26 intensity of **0.0387 kL/tonne** (38.7 litres/tonne) is significantly below this industry benchmark, which may reflect: (a) the data covering site_pune only and not the full production boundary; (b) high water recycling rates not yet captured in the data; or (c) a data completeness gap. Management should validate this figure against operational records before publication.

> **⚠ Legal/Compliance Note:** The water intensity figure of 0.0387 kL/tonne appears materially lower than sector benchmarks for integrated steel. Management must confirm this is accurate and not a result of incomplete data capture (e.g., process water recycled on-site not counted as withdrawal). Misrepresentation of water performance is a greenwashing risk.

report_sections.title: Methodology, Assumptions & Data Limitations
report_sections.body_markdown:
## Methodology, Assumptions & Data Limitations

### Reporting Standard & Boundary

- **GHG accounting standard:** GHG Protocol Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard (Scope 1 & 2); GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain Standard (Scope 3, where applicable).
- **Organisational boundary:** Operational Control (all three sites — site_pune, site_chennai, site_hq — are in scope; FY2025-26 data run covers site_pune only).
- **Reporting period:** 1 April 2025 – 31 March 2026.
- **GWP basis:** AR5 (100-year) intended; to be confirmed once emission factors are selected and applied.
- **Scope 2 accounting:** Location-based method intended; market-based method to be applied if supplier-specific emission factors or energy attribute certificates (RECs/I-RECs) are available.

### Calculation Approach

Activity data (energy consumption in kWh and litres; water withdrawal in kL) was collected from site-level operational records and validated against source documents. GHG emissions are calculated as:

`Emissions (kgCO₂e) = Activity Quantity × Emission Factor × GWP`

For this reporting period, **no emission factors were returned by the Emission Factor Database (EFDB)** for any activity row (grid electricity, diesel combustion, or municipal water supply). Per GreenMentor's data integrity policy, no emission factor has been invented, estimated, or proxied without explicit analyst approval. All GHG figures therefore remain unresolved.

### Assumptions Log

| Assumption | Basis | Impact |
|---|---|---|

1. **EFDB not wired (M2):** The Emission Factor Database service returned zero candidates for all three activity rows. This is a system configuration issue, not a data quality issue. Resolving EFDB connectivity is the single highest-priority action for completing this report.
2. **Single-site data:** All quantitative metrics in this report cover site_pune only. Chennai Plant and Mumbai HQ data have not been submitted. The report cannot be filed until all three sites are included.
3. **No prior-year baseline:** This is the first year of structured ESG data collection. No FY2024-25 baseline exists for trend analysis. Establishing a restated baseline is recommended for FY2026-27.
4. **Water intensity benchmark caveat:** The calculated water intensity of 0.0387 kL/tonne is significantly below typical integrated steel plant benchmarks (2–6 m³/tonne). Management must validate this figure before publication (see Section 8).
5. **OHS data absent:** No occupational health and safety data was submitted for any site. This is a mandatory BRSR disclosure gap.
6. **Scope 3:** Scope 3 categories have not been assessed in this data run. A Scope 3 screening exercise is recommended as a priority for FY2026-27, with at minimum Categories 1 (purchased goods), 10 (downstream processing), and 12 (end-of-life treatment) expected to be material for a steel manufacturer.

## ESG Strategy & Governance

### Strategic Intent

Acme Manufacturing Pvt Ltd is committed to embedding environmental, social, and governance (ESG) considerations into its core business strategy. As an integrated steel manufacturer operating in one of the most carbon- and resource-intensive industrial sectors, the Company recognises that its long-term licence to operate, access to capital, and customer relationships are increasingly contingent on credible, transparent, and improving ESG performance.

This FY2025-26 report marks the Company's **first-time BRSR Core filing** and establishes the baseline data infrastructure, governance accountabilities, and reporting processes that will underpin continuous improvement through FY2026-27 and beyond.

### Governance Structure

Internal data owners have been designated for each material topic and site, with data flows governed by the ESG data collection protocol established during this engagement.

### Reporting Objectives (FY2025-26)

1. Prepare and file the first-time BRSR Core report in compliance with SEBI's mandatory disclosure requirements.
2. Achieve voluntary alignment with GRI Standards to meet international stakeholder and investor expectations.
3. Map material BRSR/GRI disclosures to ESRS topical standards to future-proof reporting for European supply-chain due-diligence requirements (CSRD/CSDDD).
4. Embed TCFD-aligned climate risk and opportunity disclosures within the Annual Report narrative.
5. Establish a repeatable, audit-ready ESG data collection and assurance process across all three sites.
6. Build internal capacity — data owners, controls, and workflows — so that FY2026-27 reporting can be executed with reduced external support.

final_sections.title: Materiality Assessment
final_sections.body_markdown:
## Materiality Assessment

A structured materiality assessment was conducted to identify and prioritise the ESG topics most significant to Acme Manufacturing Pvt Ltd's business and stakeholders. Topics were scored on two axes — **Impact Score** (severity and breadth of environmental/social impact) and **Financial Score** (magnitude of financial risk or opportunity to the business) — each on a scale of 1–5. Topics scoring above the materiality threshold of **3.5** on either axis are considered material.

### Materiality Matrix Summary

| Rank | Topic | Impact Score | Financial Score | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GHG Emissions | 5 / 5 | 5 / 5 | **Material** |
| 2 | Occupational Health & Safety | 5 / 5 | 4 / 5 | **Material** |
| 3 | Water & Effluents | 4 / 5 | 4 / 5 | **Material** |

*All three topics exceed the materiality threshold of 3.5 on both axes.*

### Topic Rationales

**GHG Emissions (Rank 1 — Impact: 5, Financial: 5)**
Steel manufacturing is among the most carbon-intensive industries globally, with blast furnace–basic oxygen furnace (BF-BOF) routes emitting approximately 1.8 tCO₂ per tonne of steel. GHG emissions drive severe environmental impact through climate change and air quality degradation, and face escalating financial exposure via carbon pricing mechanisms, the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), transition risk, and investor/lender covenant requirements. BRSR Core mandates Scope 1 & 2 disclosure; TCFD scenario analysis, ESRS E1, and GRI 305 are all in scope. Sector materiality maps (GRI 11 Mining & Metals, SASB Iron & Steel) universally identify this as the highest-priority topic.

**Occupational Health & Safety (Rank 2 — Impact: 5, Financial: 4)**
Steel manufacturing is among the highest-risk industrial sectors, with hazards including molten metal handling, confined spaces, heavy machinery, and heat stress. Fatal and serious injury rates represent a direct, severe impact on workers and contractors. Financial exposure includes regulatory penalties under the Factories Act 1948 and OSHA-equivalent statutes, compensation liabilities, productivity loss, and reputational damage affecting talent attraction and customer/investor relationships. BRSR Core mandates OHS KPIs; GRI 403 and ESRS S1 apply.

**Water & Effluents (Rank 3 — Impact: 4, Financial: 4)**
Integrated steel plants are large freshwater consumers (typically 2–6 m³ per tonne of steel) and generate process effluents containing phenols, cyanides, and heavy metals from coke ovens and pickling lines. India's water-stressed geography amplifies community and regulatory risk. Financial exposure arises from water tariffs, potential regulatory shutdowns, and customer/investor ESG screens. BRSR mandates water withdrawal and discharge disclosure; GRI 303/306 and ESRS E3 apply.

### Stakeholder Engagement

The materiality questionnaire covered both internal stakeholders (management, operations, finance) and external stakeholders (investors, customers, lenders, community representatives). Key questions addressed the urgency of science-based GHG targets, carbon pricing exposure, water stress risk, effluent management, and OHS system adequacy. Full questionnaire responses are available in the supporting workpapers.

> **⚠ Placeholder — Management Review Required:** Confirm that the materiality assessment methodology and topic rankings have been reviewed and approved by the Board or designated committee before publication. Document the stakeholder engagement process (number of respondents, engagement method, dates) in the supporting annex.

## BRSR Section C — Principle 6: Water Withdrawal & Intensity
*(Corresponds to: BRSR P6 Q3 | GRI 303-3 | ESRS E3-4)*

### Performance Summary — FY2025-26

Water withdrawal data for site_pune has been validated and is reportable as a non-GHG environmental metric. The figures below are drawn directly from confirmed activity inputs.

| KPI | Value | Unit | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Total water withdrawal (municipal / third-party) | **3,250** | kL | High | Validated activity input, site_pune |
| Water intensity per tonne of production | **0.0387** | kL/tonne | High | 3,250 kL ÷ 84,000 tonnes |
| Water intensity per ₹ crore of revenue | **2.60** | kL/₹ crore | High | 3,250 kL ÷ ₹1,250 crore |

*Calculation provenance: water.intensity.production = 3,250 kL ÷ 84,000 tonnes = 0.038690 kL/tonne (rounded to 4 significant figures: 0.0387 kL/tonne). water.intensity.revenue = 3,250 kL ÷ ₹1,250 crore = 2.60 kL/₹ crore.*

> **📊 Chart ref: `chart_water_withdrawal_total_bar`** — Bar chart: Total water withdrawal (kL), site_pune, FY2025-26.

> **📊 Chart ref: `chart_water_intensity_production_line`** — Line chart: Water intensity per tonne of production (kL/tonne), site_pune, FY2025-26 (single data point; trend line to be populated in FY2026-27 with prior-year baseline).

> **📊 Chart ref: `chart_water_intensity_revenue_line`** — Line chart: Water intensity per ₹ crore of revenue (kL/₹ crore), site_pune, FY2025-26.

### Water Source Breakdown

| Source | Volume (kL) | % of Total |
|---|---|---|
| Third-party / municipal supply | 3,250 | 100% |
| Surface water | — | Not reported |
| Groundwater | — | Not reported |
| Rainwater harvesting | — | Not reported |
| Total | **3,250** | **100%** |

> **⚠ Action required (BRSR P6 Q3 / GRI 303-3):** BRSR and GRI 303-3 require disclosure of water withdrawal by **all sources** (surface water, groundwater, seawater, produced water, third-party). Confirm whether site_pune uses any sources other than municipal supply. If yes, provide volumes by source. Assign to: [Data Owner — Environment, site_pune].

### Water Discharge & Consumption

> **⚠ Placeholder:** BRSR P6 Q3 and GRI 303-4/303-5 also require water discharge volumes (by destination) and net water consumption (withdrawal minus discharge). These figures were not included in the FY2025-26 data run. Provide discharge volumes and treatment method. Assign to: [Data Owner — Environment, site_pune].

### ESRS E3-4 Note

ESRS E3-4 requires disclosure of water consumption (not just withdrawal) and, where applicable, water consumption in areas of high water stress. The 3,250 kL municipal withdrawal figure satisfies the withdrawal component. Net consumption and water-stress area classification (per WRI Aqueduct or equivalent) are required to complete ESRS E3-4.

### Contextual Note — Materiality

As noted in the materiality assessment (Section 3), water and effluents rank third among Acme's material ESG topics, with an impact score of 4/5 and a financial score of 4/5. Integrated steel plants typically consume 2–6 m³ of water per tonne of steel. Acme's FY2025-26 intensity of **0.0387 kL/tonne** (38.7 litres/tonne) is significantly below this industry benchmark, which may reflect: (a) the data covering site_pune only and not the full production boundary; (b) high water recycling rates not yet captured in the data; or (c) a data completeness gap. Management should validate this figure against operational records before publication.

> **⚠ Legal/Compliance Note:** The water intensity figure of 0.0387 kL/tonne appears materially lower than sector benchmarks for integrated steel (2–6 m³/tonne, i.e. approximately 50–150 times higher than the reported figure). Management must confirm this is accurate and not a result of incomplete data capture (e.g., process water recycled on-site not counted as withdrawal). Misrepresentation of water performance is a greenwashing risk.

## BRSR Section C — Principle 3: Occupational Health & Safety
*(Corresponds to: BRSR P3 Essential & Leadership Indicators | GRI 403-2 / 403-8 | ESRS S1-14 / S1-15)*

### ⚠ Data Gap — OHS Metrics Not Provided

Occupational Health & Safety is the **second-ranked material topic** for Acme Manufacturing Pvt Ltd (Impact: 5/5, Financial: 4/5). However, no OHS activity data was included in the FY2025-26 Phase 6 calculation run. The following metrics are required for BRSR P3 Essential Indicators and must be provided by the HR/EHS function before this report can be filed:

| Metric | Framework | Unit | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of fatalities (employees) | BRSR P3 / GRI 403-2 / ESRS S1-14 | Count | 🔴 Not provided |
| Number of fatalities (contract workers) | BRSR P3 / GRI 403-2 / ESRS S1-14 | Count | 🔴 Not provided |
| Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate — LTIFR (employees) | BRSR P3 / GRI 403-2 | Per million hours worked | 🔴 Not provided |
| LTIFR (contract workers) | BRSR P3 / GRI 403-2 | Per million hours worked | 🔴 Not provided |
| Total recordable injury rate | GRI 403-2 | Per million hours worked | 🔴 Not provided |
| Number of occupational diseases | BRSR P3 / GRI 403-2 | Count | 🔴 Not provided |
| % of workforce covered by OHS management system | GRI 403-8 / ESRS S1-14 | % | 🔴 Not provided |
| Number of OHS training hours | BRSR P3 | Hours | 🔴 Not provided |
| Near-miss incidents reported | BRSR P3 (Leadership) | Count | 🔴 Not provided |

> **⚠ Legal/Compliance Note:** BRSR P3 OHS disclosures are **mandatory Essential Indicators** under SEBI's BRSR framework. Filing without these figures is not permissible. Escalate to the EHS Head and HR Director immediately. Data must cover both permanent employees and contract workers across all three sites (site_pune, site_chennai, site_hq).

### Qualitative Context

Steel manufacturing presents significant OHS hazards including molten metal handling, confined space entry, crane and heavy-lift operations, high-temperature environments, and chemical exposure (coke oven gases, pickling acids). The Company's OHS management system, training programmes, and contractor safety oversight are critical controls. Details of these systems should be described in the BRSR Section B management disclosures (see Section 5 of this report) and validated against the requirements of GRI 403-1 (OHS management system) and ESRS S1-14.

ESRS requires a **double materiality assessment** covering both impact materiality (the company's impacts on people and environment) and financial materiality (ESG risks and opportunities affecting the company's financial position). final_sections.title: Methodology, Assumptions & Data Limitations
final_sections.body_markdown:
## Methodology, Assumptions & Data Limitations

### Reporting Standard & Boundary

- **GHG accounting standard:** GHG Protocol Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard (Scope 1 & 2); GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain Standard (Scope 3, where applicable).
- **Organisational boundary:** Operational Control (all three sites — site_pune, site_chennai, site_hq — are in scope; FY2025-26 data run covers site_pune only).
- **Reporting period:** 1 April 2025 – 31 March 2026.
- **GWP basis:** AR5 (100-year) intended; to be confirmed once emission factors are selected and applied.
- **Scope 2 accounting:** Location-based method intended; market-based method to be applied if supplier-specific emission factors or energy attribute certificates (RECs/I-RECs) are available.

### Calculation Approach

Activity data (energy consumption in kWh and litres; water withdrawal in kL) was collected from site-level operational records and validated against source documents. GHG emissions are calculated as:

`Emissions (kgCO₂e) = Activity Quantity × Emission Factor × GWP`

For this reporting period, **no emission factors were returned by the Emission Factor Database (EFDB)** for any activity row (grid electricity, diesel combustion, or municipal water supply). Per GreenMentor's data integrity policy, no emission factor has been invented, estimated, or proxied without explicit analyst approval. All GHG figures therefore remain unresolved.

### Assumptions Log

| Assumption | Basis | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Revenue = ₹1,250 crore (FY2025-26) | Provided as normalisation denominator | Used in water.intensity.revenue KPI |
| Production = 84,000 tonnes (FY2025-26) | Provided as normalisation denominator | Used in water.intensity.production KPI |
| Municipal water = 100% of water withdrawal (site_pune) | Single source reported; no other sources confirmed | May understate total withdrawal if other sources exist |
| Grid electricity = non-renewable (assumed) | No renewable energy certificate or on-site generation data provided | Renewable share = 0% assumed; to be confirmed |
| Scope 2 basis = location-based | No market-based EF or energy attribute certificate available | Market-based figure cannot be calculated |
| GWP basis = AR5 | Standard BRSR/GRI practice for Indian reporters | To be confirmed when EFs are selected |

### Data Limitations & Caveats

1. **EFDB not wired (M2):** The Emission Factor Database service returned zero candidates for all three activity rows. This is a system configuration issue, not a data quality issue. Resolving EFDB connectivity is the single highest-priority action for completing this report.
2. **Single-site data:** All quantitative metrics in this report cover site_pune only. Chennai Plant and Mumbai HQ data have not been submitted. The report cannot be filed until all three sites are included.
3. **No prior-year baseline:** This is the first year of structured ESG data collection. No FY2024-25 baseline exists for trend analysis. Establishing a restated baseline is recommended for FY2026-27.
4. **Water intensity benchmark caveat:** The calculated water intensity of 0.0387 kL/tonne is significantly below typical integrated steel plant benchmarks (2–6 m³/tonne). Management must validate this figure before publication (see Section 8).
5. **OHS data absent:** No occupational health and safety data was submitted for any site. This is a mandatory BRSR disclosure gap.
6. **Scope 3:** Scope 3 categories have not been assessed in this data run. A Scope 3 screening exercise is recommended as a priority for FY2026-27, with at minimum Categories 1 (purchased goods), 10 (downstream processing), and 12 (end-of-life treatment) expected to be material for a steel manufacturer.

### Assurance

> **⚠ Placeholder — Management Decision Required:** BRSR Core requires reasonable assurance on specified KPIs from a SEBI-registered assurance provider. Confirm the assurance scope, provider, and timeline. Note that unresolved GHG figures cannot be assured until emission factors are applied and calculations completed.
